sourceMovie.title: To Live and Die in L.A.
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: To Live and Die in L.A. (1985) stuck because it commits to its crime premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. William Friedkin's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The crime and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Point Break
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If To Live and Die in L.A. clicked for you, Point Break (1991) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after To Live and Die in L.A.?
faq.answer: Try Point Break, Lethal Weapon 3, The Jackal—each shares DNA with To Live and Die in L.A. while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is To Live and Die in L.A.?
faq.answer: To Live and Die in L.A. (1985) is best described as Crime, Thriller, Action. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is To Live and Die in L.A. worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
